strengths: Novel application of truth maintenance to agent memory
Clear problem framing: truthfulness and abstention in long-horizon memory
Structured claim schema with validity intervals and edges
Strong empirical gains on abstention and single-session-user slices
Ablation study isolates causal role of LLM linker
weaknesses: Overall accuracy is lower than baseline (61.3% vs 68.0%)
Method details are high-level; lacks algorithmic specifics
No statistical significance reported for main results
Small sample sizes for key slices (e.g., n=9 for abstention)
No comparison to recent state-of-the-art memory systems
Placeholder numbers and macros throughout (e.g., efficiency table)
required_revisions: Replace all placeholder numbers (e.g., 13603, 35904 tokens) with actual experimental results.
Provide full algorithm details for maximum-consistent subgraph and edge labeling.
Report statistical significance (p-values, confidence intervals) for all comparisons.
Clarify the experimental protocol: how was the 150-question slice selected?
Include a detailed comparison to recent memory baselines beyond Flat RAG and A-Mem.
Explain the regression on assistant-authored, multi-session, and preference slices.
Provide full implementation details and hyperparameter tuning process.
Add a table with complete results for all question types, not just selected slices.

strengths: Novel idea: truth maintenance for agent memory via claim-level graph with explicit edges.
Clear problem framing: addresses abstention and knowledge-update failures in long-horizon memory.
Efficiency claim: session-batched writer reduces token cost vs. per-turn baseline.
weaknesses: Incomplete results: overall accuracy is lower than baseline (-6.7pp).
Missing statistical significance: no p-values or confidence intervals for main claims.
Insufficient ablation: only one ablation (linker removal) is reported.
Unclear evaluation: stratified slice size (150) not justified; no details on stratification.
Placeholder numbers: macros not filled (e.g., token counts, latency).
No failure analysis: errors on non-target slices (SSA, MS) not analyzed.
Reproducibility gaps: missing dataset preprocessing details (e.g., haystack capping).
required_revisions: Fill all placeholder macros with actual numbers (e.g., token counts, latency).
Report statistical significance (e.g., McNemar p-values, confidence intervals) for all slice comparisons.
Add ablation studies: test contribution of claim schema, temporal parser, and hybrid reader separately.
Justify the 150-question stratified slice: show it is representative of full benchmark.
Analyze failures: explain performance drop on SSA and MS slices.
Clarify evaluation protocol: detail how LLM judge is applied and how abstention is scored.
Provide full hyperparameter search details and sensitivity analysis.
Include runtime and memory usage details for full system.

strengths: Clear problem framing: truth maintenance for long-horizon agents
Novel integration of truth maintenance with LLM-based linking
Strong, specific results on abstention and single-session-user slices
Efficiency claims: lower token cost than baseline A-Mem
weaknesses: Overall accuracy is lower than baseline (61.3% vs 68.0%)
Performance regresses on several non-target slices (e.g., SSA, MS)
Scalability of O(k^2) MCS algorithm and edge storage unclear for large graphs
Relies heavily on LLM linker; robustness to linker errors not analyzed
No analysis of latency/throughput under high write load
required_revisions: Clarify the 150-question stratified slice selection method and justify its representativeness.
Provide token/latency breakdown (write vs. read) to validate efficiency claims.
Discuss system limitations: e.g., handling of ambiguous or complex claims, scalability of graph operations.
Include placeholder values for missing macros (e.g., \tau_{\text{sim}}, \tau_{\text{bypass}}) or state they are tuned hyperparameters.
Justify the choice of DeepSeek-V3.2 for all LLM calls and discuss cost/performance trade-offs.
